original: [var0 |You fetch Paulette’s drawing and show it to Monika. “Do you know something about this logo?” The kaithrit barmaid squints, zooming in with her glowing blue eye. “I’ve seen it before, when we were looking for a place to open our bar. We didn’t stay long when Kiki looked into its tightly-locked intranet. You don’t wanna know what’s inside, trust me.” So she knows where it is? Monika nods. “Yes. It’s an abandoned factory at the far end of the Gyre — the last building before the sunward side of the city meets the sun-scarred steppes. Why do you want to go there?” “To help someone close,” you simply answer. “I see. There’s no reasoning with you then? They must be very important to be worth risking your life against experimental cyborgs and psycho warriors,” she says, reaching for her QComm-device. “Kiki? Can you come quickly? [pc.name] needs the E.G.G. data.” What was that about cyborgs and psionics? “Kiki will explain it better than I. The only thing I know is that the place drains huge amounts of energy from the power grid — a million times more than the bar does.” “Exactly 3.2 million times more,” Kiki adds before claiming the seat right next to you. “This isn’t your garden variety foundry, it’s a massive industrial complex. I’m surprised they don’t have their own reactor.” Sounds like the place isn’t as abandoned as it’s supposed to be. The dusky techie projects a 3D rendering of the E.G.G. on her holopad. “There’s only one entrance. It’ll surely be heavily guarded by a strong zaika — maybe XOs or something meaner. There’s been leaks in the ventilation system, lots of corridors are locked down, but the main rooms are still operational, according to the latest reports.” What can you expect inside? “Before they shut the factory down and purged the workers, they received a huge shipment of captured leyaks as well as a tanker full of chrome: segmented legs, sensors, targeting systems, drug injectors, monomolecular blades, and so on. The waiver also mentions a telepath mercenary — certainly a new manager or safety officer. The rest of the data was too heavily-encrypted to be cracked with my tools, but I have one last clue.” And it is? Kiki types something on the keyboard. A moment later, large tanks appear on screen. “There’s thousands of cryogenic chambers stored in the lowest level. I don’t know who or what they hold, but it can’t be good. Be careful [pc.name], you’re not dealing with a small fry.” “I will. Thank you Kiki — and you too Monika.” The [silly |milfy |mature ] kaithrit smiles. “It’s the least we can do after you brought the Mastodon back in one piece. I hope you’ll find what you’re looking for.” You have a hunch that you will. For now, Paulette will want to hear what you’ve learned. |You fetch Paulette’s drawing and show it to Monika. “Do you know something about this logo?” The kaithrit barmaid squints, zooming in with her glowing blue eye. “I’ve seen it before, when we were looking for a place to open our bar. You found a clue to the GU headquarters’ whereabouts. Report back to Paulette!

A cluster of Dhaal’s idle youth squat in a darkened alcove, hunched around something flickering and bluish. Looking closer, you discover a game of hardlight dice, though modified with projected obstacles and events to spice up the already competitive game. One young zaika doesn’t partake. Instead, she clutches a miniaturized projector in a hand laced with glowing circuitry. She must be running the game.

A large section of wall across the street from the youths is filled with half-scrapped mechanicals and other urban waste. The word ‘MOO’ is painted near it, in a curling script. As you walk past, you catch sight of a wide, arched doorway partly hidden behind the junk.
The street stretches north and south through foggy twilight.
Doorway
Doorway

The abandoned station looks the same as before. Yellow lights flicker above the stairway that leads to the Gyre, and the platform is empty except for scattered junk. Maybe there’s a new penis drawn on the wall, maybe not.
The door to the Crash Landing is closed, but you can head over. Other than that, there’s nowhere to go but out.
Door
Leave

The Crash Landing’s entry hall looks like it was once mostly concrete, but there are sheets of metal bolted across the walls now, along with steel beams holding up the ceiling. Some of them look supportive, others more decorative. Blue and yellow lights shine among all the metal, pulsing with the beat of the distant music. It’s dim, but not threatening, and promises a good time at the other end of the darkness.

The thraggen woman, Karrah, stands to one side, watching you from next to the enormous steel door she’s guarding. Some kind of scanner unit is bolted to the wall next to her, probably how she works the hardlight display outside the door. You remember what she said about stunners set up for security, and look up. Thin pipes run up and down the length of the ceiling, with mechanical stuff bolted onto them here and there. You’re not sure what most of it is, but there could be stunners in there somewhere.
Karrah herself looks bored, but watches you with one hand on the stun baton at her hip, like she’s waiting for you to make a move now that you’re inside. You could chat her up, or head for the bar.

You walk into a large room with metal and concrete walls, lit by yellow and blue globes of light on the ceiling. The globes slowly fade from dim to brighter, making the room’s shadows fade and deepen in turn. Booths line the walls all around the room, filled with zaika in various levels of blissed-out on milk, talking and flirting and just sitting there staring at the lights. Hardlight displays and flickering monitors line the walls, showing news and sports and porn and whatever else the patrons want to see.

The bar sits at the back of the longest wall, a long steel horseshoe shape with about a dozen stools in front of it. More zaika sit on the stools, though some of them are swaying in place, their tails wrapped around the stools’ posts. Two women stand behind the bar, glasses in their hands as they work. One of them is a purple-haired kaithrit, and the other looks human but has bovine ears and a tail.

There’s a low rumble of conversation and laughter throughout the whole place, but at the same time, nobody really seems comfortable here. It feels like they know the bar is hidden for a reason, and with the stranglehold the corporations have on the city above, this place could get raided at any time just for being a different place to get milk. So it doesn’t look like anybody’s getting too wild, just drinking and trying to relax.

Another doorway opens to the west, rimmed with steel and lights glowing in all different colors. The pounding music is louder from that way, and through the doorway, you can see a dance floor filled with gyrating zaika, along with a pool table.
You could head to the bar and talk to one of the women there, or check out the dance and pool room.

You head into the bar’s next room, and a thumping bass line hits you right in the chest. There are some powerful speakers embedded in the walls here, lining the ceiling and behind grates near the floor. Flashing and fading lights of all colors and shapes shine from the walls, moving with the beat of the music, making the entire place feel like you’ve stepped into some kind of old-school club out of a rebellious science fiction novel.

Dozens of zaika shimmy and shake on the dance floor, a throbbing sea of sweaty bodies and shining hair and tails shivering back and forth. With the shifting lights and the dancing crowd, it’s hard to tell who’s where for more than a second or two, but everyone seems lost in the music, probably high on milk and glad for the chance to party.

On the other side of the room stand two chunks of equipment with hardlight mounts on them. Whatever they once were, they’ve been jury-rigged into pool tables, with small hardlight projection rods hanging from the sides. A few zaika play pool at one table, using the hardlight rods as cues. Some other clearly-improvised hardlight projectors create dartboards on the walls, though there’s nobody playing there right now.

A human woman with skin about as dark as the zaika stands in one corner, watching over the place from behind a pair of glasses with mismatched lenses - one round, one rectangular. She’s wearing a dark purple vest and slacks that look like something from an old Earth movie, complete with a long-sleeved white shirt and a bowtie. The vest barely stays closed over her sizable chest. Her wavy blue hair trails down past her shoulders, and her eyes dart back and forth, like she’s trying to keep an eye on the whole room at once. Long, pointed ears stick out from beneath her hair.
When the lights shift to shine on her, you catch sight of an enormous bulge between her legs. Whatever’s down there, she’s got a lot of it.

original: “You. Sweetpea.” The chubby kaithrit takes a sharp step towards you, raises her hand and then halts, eyes wide, her usually soft expression tight with emotion. She looks unsure what to say or do. “They — they put this collar on me and told me my contract had been bought, but they wouldn’t say by who,” Dizzy says, grip digging into the Paragon rainproof thrown over her shoulders. “I did think — but usually when that happens it’s by big shot pirates or execs for private collections, and so — but it’s you.” She stares at you plaintively. “Why?” You weren’t willing to bring Bizzy to this snakepit of a planet to see her, so you’re doing the next best thing: you’re bringing her to see Bizzy. Buying her out was a necessity in order to fulfill your promise. Dizzy starts to cry, inky mascara crawling silently down her round cheeks. She fumbles around in her chemise and dabs at the damage with a crumpled up tissue. “The antenna was supposed to stop these sorts of dreams,” she mumbles, turning her wet marble eyes up to the pink-tinted Dhaal sky. “They’re worse than anything else.” “It’s not a dream. Look.” You step into her and give her a firm hug, pressing yourself into the pillowy give. “Doesn’t that feel real? You’re my employee now. And if there’s one thing I can promise, I’m not Beth Carver.” After a moment she squeezes you back, nestles her face in your shoulder and sobs a couple of times. “I’m sorry,” she sniffs, breaking away to brush at her rapidly-dissolving make-up again. “It’s — this is just the nicest thing anyone’s ever done for me.” “C’mon,” you smile at her, nodding at the boarding ramp. “Let’s get you onboard.” Dizzy will remain on your ship until the next time you visit Bizzy.

original: Her body is thin and wiry, but rather than appearing frail, you get the impression of surprising strength being compacted into her diminutive form. Her chest is on the smaller side, and can’t be much larger than a B-cup, while the line of her waist and hips is visible but not too pronounced. Though her body isn’t as traditionally curvy as most other women’s, her face is unmistakably feminine. The girlishness of her features stand out even without any makeup.

Her jacket is made of a tough, leathery looking material, colored a surprisingly soft shade of mauve. Though cropped at the waist, its sleeves are full length, albeit pushed up to just below her elbows. It features a stiff, wide collar that juts straight up. The entire jacket itself is covered in various zippered pockets, big and small. They look like they’re filled with all sorts of useful things. Underneath the jacket is a charcoal-colored v-neck t-shirt.
You know from experience that underneath that is a lacy black bralette, which can be easily pushed away to reveal two perky pink nipples.

Her pants are made of black leather and skintight, held in place with a pair of overlapping belts, one of which hangs a little more loosely on her hip than the other. On the upper belt hangs a series of pockets and various tools, much like how her jacket seems to be stuffed with goodies. A third belt encircling her leg is equipped with a holster containing a large pistol. The right leg of her pants has been cropped short to show off a sliver of thigh, only for the rest of the limb to be covered by a thigh-high stocking. Both feet are laced up in well-worn combat boots with a sensible inch-high heel.
“Enjoying the show?” Evening asks, already done with her own inspection. “My eyes are up here, buddy.” They’re scolding words, but she’s clearly joking.

You lean over to tell Evening over the din of the bar that there’s something you’ve been wanting to ask. She rests her chin over laced fingers and considers what you have to say.
“Mmm, let me guess, you have a very particular set of questions, the answers to which I’ve acquired over a very long career? I think I can help with that.”
“Sure; I’m always up for a good conversation. What would you like to know?”
You chat a bit about Dhaal, the Zaika, and the strip, before you bring up the unconventional welcome you got when you touched down for the first time.
“You too, huh?” Evening swirls her drink about in her glass. “I had a bit of a rough landing myself.”
“Rebels storm your shuttle too?”

She waves the idea down. “Oh no, I meant the literal landing. My ship was in pretty bad shape when I first got here; I bet a hard knock on the hull would have collapsed it completely. I was lucky to land when I did, or else I might have been a goner.” The prospect doesn’t seem to perturb the woman too much, because she’s all smiles as she says this. “Don’t worry, I have a talent for making it by the skin of my teeth.”
You ask what could have possibly happened to damage her ship that badly.
“Well,” Evening begins to explain. “A pirate ship accosted me on the way here.”
“Oof! Bad luck there.”
“That tracks. Those fuckers are everywhere, aren’t they?”
“As they do.”

“Right. And obviously, I opened fire, but my ship... "+({var0}?"Well, you’ve seen my ship. I love it, but it’s kind of a piece of junk.":"It’s not the most robust in design. Or structural integrity. The Theseus has been with me through hell and high water, but by Void, it shows.")+"” The mercenary sighs. “One day I’d like to give her a nice upgrade or two, maybe replace the wall with the giant hole I had to patch up, but that day isn’t coming any time soon. I’ve already spent the last couple of weeks fixing her back up to a livable state... thank Void for that pod hotel down the Gyre.”

The Theseus has been with me through hell and high water, but by Void, it shows.")+"” The mercenary sighs. “One day I’d like to give her a nice upgrade or two, maybe replace the wall with the giant hole I had to patch up, but that day isn’t coming any time soon. I’ve already spent the last couple of weeks fixing her back up to a livable state... thank Void for that pod hotel down the Gyre.”")

original: “So, the pirates?” You gently nudge her story back on track.

“Oh, right. Well, they boarded the ship, and this was usually the part where they march in, demand your credits, or rob you for everything you’re worth, right? The problem was, I was really tight on credits, and I didn’t have much worth stealing. Except my gun collection.”
“How big is your collection?”
“Seventy-three pieces and counting, not including the extra scopes, grips, and other spare parts, etcetera.”
“You don’t think that’s too-”
“Nope,” Evening chirps. “I love each and every one of them, and I will die before I let a pirate lay his filthy pirating hands on a single one. So obviously getting robbed wasn’t an option for me.”
“So, let me guess,” you interject, leaning on the bar as you make your brilliant deduction. “The ship got damaged because you fought them off?”
“You could say that.” The Terran’s brow crinkles, in a way that isn’t quite a frown. “More like, I scared them off.”
“How so?”
“Well, I... may have mildly blown up The Theseus. Multiple times.”
That’s not what you expected. At all. “Wh- How do you mildly blow up a spaceship?” you balk. The fact that she’s so nonchalant about this is a mind-boggling impossibility to you right now.

“Well, if you want to get technical about it: very carefully,” the mercenary explains. “I blocked off the entrances to force them into a certain hallway, and set off a few small, mechanical explosions. You know, so nothing caught on fire. Then I would have really been fucked. I had a couple of smaller bombs left over from a job I did, so now seemed like as good a time as any to use them up. They tried to go into the rec room. Bomb. They opened a closet door. Bomb. They tried to break down a door I barricaded. By the time they got through, the room was already been laced with-”
“More bombs?”
“You guessed it. And some mild tear gas, for extra fun.”
“You didn’t just shoot them?” "+({var0}.isMischievous()?"You can’t believe you’re thinking this, but the":"The")+" relentless bombing of your enemies sounds like the tiniest bit of overkill.
context: <<POS:02010971>> <<VARS:var0=pc>> output("\n\n“You didn’t just shoot them?” "+(pc.isMischievous()?"You can’t believe you’re thinking this, but the":"The")+" relentless bombing of your enemies sounds like the tiniest bit of overkill.")

original: The mercenary shakes her head. “Explosives were okay because they had a certain radius of damage, and the ones I used weren’t actually that large. Think somewhere between a cherry bomb and a grenade. But bullets go far, and they ricochet. There’s too much of a risk of one bouncing around and piercing something integral. The pirates probably knew that too, because they didn’t fire any shots either. It was either that or the fact that I cut all the lights beforehand.”
“Wow,” you say with
mild shock.
begrudging admiration.
barely hidden incredulity.
“You’d rather destroy your entire ship and risk getting jettisoned into space than let a couple of thieves touch your stuff?”

“Hey now, it was very important stuff to me, so yes,” Evening tries to explain a little defensively. The expression on your face doesn’t exactly say you’ve been convinced, so she rubs the bridge of her nose and tries again. “Look, I’m not exactly what you would call ‘physically gifted’. Or ‘intellectually gifted’. Or even ‘above average’ in a lot of respects. Not compared to some other species. So when you’re facing off against an enemy who might have you trumped in one or both regards, your best options are to shoot them from far away or make them explode from far away. Or, if it’s dark and they’re in unfamiliar territory, sneak up on them and strangle them a bit from really close up. But that’s neither here nor there. And if you have to take a little risk for a high reward, I’m willing to take it. I have my priorities straight.”
“I think we have very different definitions of ‘straight’.”

This makes Evening chuckle. “The good thing for me was, I knew my ship. I was on it when I first left Terra, and I’ve been with it ever since. I know exactly how much abuse it can take, and where, before it gets compromised, and they didn’t. So when the walls started shaking and this hissing sound started coming through the pipes, they all packed up and got the fuck out, thinking they only had a few minutes until the entire craft was going to explode. Joke’s on them. It actually had two whole hours.” Evening throws back her drink and sighs a satisfied kind of sigh. “It was enough for me to set a course for the nearest civilised planet. And that’s how I landed on Dhaal for the first time.”
“That was...” You pause and think of the words to say. “
Very risky sounding. And you came out okay?
Pretty epic. But I’m also still surprised you’re still alive.
The most reckless thing I’ve heard in awhile. How are you still alive?
The mercenary only shrugs. “Skin of my teeth, remember? Worked out for me so far.”
Several drinks later...
“So what do you know about planet races?” Evening asks you.

Your brows crinkle as you consider the question. It sounds pretty obvious from the name, but you get the feeling that this is one of those things that don’t turn out to be anything like what they sound. “Is it a race between planets?” After all, it sounds pretty similar to a Planet Rush, and you definitely know what that is.

“Close,” the mercenary declares. “It’s a race on a planet. The idea is that you have to fly close enough that your spacecraft is affected by the planet’s atmosphere, and occasionally the odd landmark or two. Depending on how big a race, it can take place over multiple planets, where contestants get eliminated if they don’t place high or fast enough. Some of them are pretty serious, with huge prize pools and everything. They’re supposed to be tests of endurance and adaptability, but over the years a lot of them have devolved into opportunities to cheat your way to the top. Usually by murdering the competition.”
(0!=(1&flags.EVENING_TALKS)?" “Oh yeah, I think you mentioned that before,” you say with a nod. ":" ")+"“And that’s where you come in?”
context: <<POS:02015262>> output((0!=(1&flags.EVENING_TALKS)?" “Oh yeah, I think you mentioned that before,” you say with a nod. ":" ")+"“And that’s where you come in?”")

original: “Yeah, that’s where I come in.” Evening smirks. “There was a small race held not too long ago, on a dusty, rocky planet called Cularia. I was hired by a couple – nice people, really into engineering. One was a pilot, the other was an engineer, and I was brought on to be a gunner. It was an easy job at first glance; as long as no one tried any funny business, I wouldn’t even have to do anything, just putter around the ship and not touch anything that looked important. We weren’t exactly what you would call big fish, so most likely we’d just finish the race somewhere between fifth and tenth place, and not have to deal with anyone trying to shoot us out of the sky while we did it.”
“Let me guess,” you interject. “It wasn’t that easy.”

The woman snorts. “I wouldn’t be telling this story if it was. No, the good news for my clients was that the new engine they were testing out performed way beyond their expectations, so we actually started off in the lead. We had a real chance of actually winning this thing. The bad news was that this put us right on top of everyone’s shitlist. As soon as second place caught up to us, they started opening fire. If you’ve ever been in a fire fight-”
“Oh, I have,” you interject.

“-then you know how this goes. I shoot, they shoot, eventually one of us goes down. I, being excellent at my job, manage to smoke their asses and send them nose-diving to ground level. But then...” She sighs at this, rubbing the back of her neck.
You lean in. You know that’s not the end of it. “But?”
“But then they shot us down at the same time,” Evening finishes. Another second sigh escapes her. “Always another complication.”
You gasp, and tell her that she’s being awfully cavalier about crash landing on the surface of a strange planet.

“Ah, we landed okay enough, thanks to the pilot,” she says, waving off the supposed danger. “It was bumpy and hella loud, but everyone got strapped in in time for the impact so no one got hurt. The bigger problem was the fact that our competition had landed nearby. So while everyone else was zooming past way over our heads, the mechanic had to rush to fix the ship while our attackers could decide to bring the fight to ground level at any moment. Which they did, almost immediately, because they were dicks like that. One second, we’re trying to fix a hole in the hull, and the next, someone’s lobbed a grenade into said hole. It was very rude.”
You ask how she got out of that unscathed.

“Grabbed the grenade, threw it back out, ducked. Didn’t have a lot of time to think, just act. It didn’t explode in my face, so thankfully I was fast enough, but I still had to deal with the unwelcome guests outside. The pilot and I ended up rushing outside to meet them, three big beefy fuckers with the meanest mugs you’d have ever seen. They fired shots, we fired shots. One of them charged at me with a sword, and I had to dodge sideways and knock him out before he could hit me or the hull again. We had to hold them all off and prevent them from attacking the ship at the same time. I used up more smoke grenades than I would have liked that day. And when that smoke cleared-” here Evening tuts between her teeth, and sighs a deep sigh. “The fuckers were down, but so was my pilot.”
You gasp again. “You mean...”

“He wasn’t dead, but he definitely wasn’t in condition to fly,” she clarifies. “So I hoisted him back onto the ship and made a beeline for the other spacecraft. We didn’t have any medikits on board, so my only hope was snatching one off of the other team instead. Luckily for me, the three fucksterteers were the only hired muscle they had, so by the time I broke onto their ship, they didn’t have any defense against me and my multiple guns and explosives.”
“So what did you do to them?”

“Not much. This was still a race, so I had to hurry. Just took some of their medical supplies, smacked them around a bit, and jammed a few grenades into their engine room. Probably blew up half their ship, and whatever equipment they had. Standard payback stuff.”
“Sounds about right,” you say with a sagely nod.
Being a mercenary yourself, you know the unspoken laws that come with work like that.
It’s about what you would have done if you were in her shoes.
After all, anyone who tries to fuck you over so blatantly deserves whatever comes to them, even if that whatever is you.
“So, what happened next?”

You startle a bit. Payback or not, that sounds a little brutal. You try not to let on how unnerving you find the idea, but Evening catches on quickly enough. “Oh, don’t look at me like that,” she signs, morose but not moroseful. “Stuff like this happens all the time. You have to make hard choices, maybe even cruel ones, but going soft could make you lose anything from a contract to your life. I did what I had to do to fulfill the contract.”
“Did you... kill them?” you ask uneasily.

Evening blinks at you before replying hastily, “well, no. I still have a line I won’t cross. I’m not about to commit genocide on a planet full of babies or anything. But if getting the job done requires a little blackmail, torture, arson, or... whatever the exploding version of arson is, then you gotta do what you gotta do.”
You relax slightly, though her affinity with apparently exploding her enemies is a bit alarming to hear about. “So, what happened next?”
“Damn,” you say with a laugh, partly impressed but also partly unnerved. “That’s hardcore.”

Evening shrugs. “Comes with the territory of the job. If I’d let them go unscathed, they might have gone after us, and we’d have to do the same shit all over again. I was already down one crew member, and there were already only three of us to start with. Didn’t want anyone else almost dying on me.”
“You didn’t... you know?” You slice a thumb across your neck in pantomime.

Evening shakes her head. “Nah. The grenades wrecked their ship, but unless any of them tried to run towards the explosion, they should have been fine. And if they did, then at that point they’re just asking to die.”
You raise an eyebrow, but otherwise don’t say anything more on the matter. “Fair enough. So what happened next?”

“We didn’t win, for one thing,” the woman laments. “But we finished the race. The pilot survived. I got paid. It wasn’t the most successful of missions in my book, but you gotta learn to take the good with the bad, right? I will say, those guys taught me a thing or two about evasive maneuvers. Comes in handy when I run into the errant space pirate every so often.”
Several drinks later...
The conversation eventually turns to Evening’s overstuffed utility belt.
“What’s in there, anyways?” you ask. There are so many random compartments and objects hanging off of it that you
admit you’re dying to know what at least a few of them are.
can’t help but wonder what kinds of goodies she has hidden in there.
have to wonder whether she actually uses half of the stuff on a regular basis, or if she’s just paranoid.

Evening taps her chin in thought. “Weeeeell, I’m not one to give up all my secrets, but I’ll tell you a few of them off of the top of my head,” she decides. After a few moments of pondering, she rattles off: “smoke grenades, flash grenades, gas grenades, sound grenades, EMP grenades, actual grenades, extra ammo, ear plugs, regelatin spray, immuno-boosters, grav cuffs, a mirror, a hardlight dagger, mag blinders, a grappling hook,
kryptonite, shark repellent, a whole-ass jetpack, a make-up kit, crayons, exotic butters,
and my guns, obviously. Oh, and string.”
“String?”
“String.”
“Just string?”
“Very strong string.”
“Hmm.” You roll over the list in your head. “No aphrodisiac grenades?” They’d certainly round out her line-up of throwable exploding things.

“Uh- well-” Evening splutters, put on the spot. Having suddenly lost the ability to look directly into your eyes, the Terran’s gaze starts sidling off into the distance as she wrings her hands. “I used to carry them around, but... there was an incident. And now it’s in my best interest not to use them anymore.” Her face is glowing a fascinating shade of red.
Your eyes narrow. “So, what happened?”

The mercenary’s mouth opens and closes like a fish before she bites her lip and averts her eyes again. Her cheeks get progressively redder as your gaze burrows further into her, despite her hemming and hawing in an attempt to disinterest you in the subject. She doesn’t want to talk, but you’re too curious to let it drop, putting you at a stalemate.

Eventually her silence breaks. Kind of. “Yeah, I’m not nearly drunk enough to be able to-” Evening starts to say, before you immediately flag down "+({var0}?"Monika":"the bartender")+" and order three Rex Pistols for her.
context: <<POS:02025078>> <<VARS:var0=void 0!==flags.MONIKA_MET>> output("\n\nEventually her silence breaks. Kind of. “Yeah, I’m not nearly drunk enough to be able to-” Evening starts to say, before you immediately flag down "+(void 0!==flags.MONIKA_MET?"Monika":"the bartender")+" and order three Rex Pistols for her.")

original: Evening looks your way, picks up one of the tall, thin glasses, looks at you again, and deflates visibly with a sigh. “You’re not going to let me go until I talk, are you?”
“Well, you don’t have to,” you say in the most saintly tone you can muster, “but it’d be the nice thing to do after getting all this free alcohol, right?”
“Hell no,” you say with a cheerful grin. “So come on, dish! It can’t be that bad; we’ve all got some embarrassing stories to tell.”
“Nope,” you say tersely. “So you may as well spill the beans now.”
Another sigh. “Okay, here goes nothing,” she mutters, before pouring the first of the pale, bubbly drinks down the hatch.
By the time she finishes the third glass, she’s definitely drunk enough to talk.

“Look,” the now very inebriated Terran ends up explaining while trying to slur her words as little as possible. “I’m a professional. And I know the definition of ‘professional’ varies greatly between planets, but for me it includes not fucking anyone in the middle of a job. Or for a job. I know it’s different for most people around these parts, ‘specially this far from the core; s’just how I feel personally. But there was this one guy...”
“Go on.”

The woman swallows hard before continuing. “His name was Sieg. Tall, muscular, could probably pick me up and snap me in half if he wanted. Scariest looking ausar I’ve ever seen, but luckily he was on my side because we had the same empli- empler- we were contracted by the same dude.”
You nod, not wanting to interrupt vocally.

“We were hired to get... uh...” Evening’s brow crinkles as she tries and fails to recall the details. “Something. From a pirate ship. I don’t remember. Anyways, it was supposed to be a stealth mission, but Sieg and his huge, hunky body ended up tripping an alarm. We couldn’t afford to get surrounded, so he picks me up and starts sprinting down the hallway, with, like, fifteen pirates on our tail. I still have my pistol so I’m picking them off while he carries me, and somehow we shake them off long enough to hide in one of their... I don’t know, closets? This... shed? We just ran into a room and shut the door behind us, but it turned out to be this small dead end so Sieg slammed me into the wall by accident. And it didn’t hurt that much, but one of the aphro grenades on my belt must have fallen off or gotten hit by the impact because it exploded, and the gas filled up the whole room and we had to breathe it all in and... and...”
You finish the sentence for her. “You had sex.”

“Yeah, we had sex, and it was really good sex but also a really bad idea because there were still a fuckload of pirates around and if we’d gotten caught we would have been in really deep shit,” Evening howls. “Thank fuck we snapped out of it in time, because another group of them came ‘round the corner right after.”
A smirk creeps onto your face. You can’t resist tormenting her a bit. “You think they knew?”
“Probably,” she whimpers, burying her face in her hands. “We were so loud.”
You can’t help but

giggle discreetly at the idea, though not quite discreetly enough for Evening not to notice. “Did you at least make it out okay?” You try to sound as sympathetic as possible, even though just visualizing the situation makes you want to laugh again.

guffaw as conspicuously as possible, which only makes Evening’s face crumple harder in distress. “Hey, trust me when I say we’ve all been there. Trust me. And at least you made it out alive, right?” you say in an attempt to get her to see the bright side of things.
half scoff, half laugh at the idea. “Well, did you at least manage to unfuck your fuck-up?”

“Yeah. I think.” The mercenary rakes her hands through her hair before slumping onto the bar. “I don’t even remember how. The next few minutes are kinda blurry. But we got the loot and got paid, so it worked out fine. Somehow.”
“So, that was it?” You prod her side with your elbow as you "+({var0}.isAss()?"raise an eyebrow":"shoot a cheeky grin")+" at her. “For you and Sieg.”
context: <<POS:02029789>> <<VARS:var0=pc>> output("\n\n“So, that was it?” You prod her side with your elbow as you "+(pc.isAss()?"raise an eyebrow":"shoot a cheeky grin")+" at her. “For you and Sieg.”")

original: Cue the nervous laughter. “Hahaha, nooooo, we fucked five more times afterwards. Or six. I lost count.” The woman fidgets in her seat. “We actually teamed up a bunch more after that. Made a good pair. Good times.”
“Where’s this Sieg now?”
you inquire, batting your eyelashes. If he’s as hunky as Evening makes him out to be, you definitely want to get an eyeful of him ASAP.
If he’s as buff as Evening makes him out to be, you definitely want to see how he stacks up.
The Terran shrugs. “Dunno. Elsewhere. We drifted apart after a bit. Haven’t been in contact in a while.” The question seems to have sobered her up a bit.

Several gulps of cream liquor later, Evening is tapping her chin, looking thoughtful. “Here’s a good one,” she says. “I was asked to bring in a guy who was just about to go on the run. White-collar jackass, corporate worker. You know the type.”
“Ohhhh, what’d he do? Dish!”
“Yep, totally know ‘em. So what’d he do?”
“What’d he do?”
“You could say that. So what’d he do?”
“Don’t remind me. What kind of shit did he pull?”
you ask.

“Ehhhhh.” Apparently what he did wasn’t very important because Evening makes a vague gesture with her hand and doesn’t give much more information than that. “Something white-collar and involving the corporation he worked for. I’m a little fuzzy on the details. The moral of this story is that rich assholes can usually get away with a lot of stuff, but they’re screwed if they ever end up pissing off another-”
“Rich asshole,” you both say in unison, to which Evening snaps her fingers. “Yeah, you got it.”

“Anyways.” She takes another sip of her drink and continues the story. “Whatever this guy did, the group that hired me was absolutely livid. Wanted me to take him in or take him down, whatever means necessary, no questions asked. Thing is, their noses weren’t much cleaner than his was, which is why they went to me instead of any law enforcement. I promised them I could get it done quietly. A demonstration of my particular skills helped seal the deal.” She mimes firing off a gun, a smirk on her face.
“So how did you get him?”

“I did a little digging, and found out that whatever he did involved some data he stole. Whether he was going to exploit it or sell it off, I’m not sure, but I knew for a fact that some of it was still at his office. Whether he didn’t have enough time to get it all the first time, or he just missed it, I’m not sure. Either way, it was from a private network, so he had to be physically present to take it. ‘Return to the scene of the crime’, and all that jazz. I knew he would have to come during the night, so I camped in the office building to wait for him.”
“And he came?”

“Yup. Even used his old company badge to get in. I’m not sure if he was stupid or if he just had humongous balls. Either way, I was waiting for him in his office. Caught him with his pants down, metaphorically speaking. I got my gun trained on him and him dead to rights. My employers’ preferences were to bring him in alive, so I tried to talk him into coming quietly, but you know what he does?”
You lean in forward, wanting to know. “What?”
“He takes his shirt off.”
“...” That’s not what you expected at all. “But why?”

“That’s what I was wondering! I had no fucking idea. Next thing I know he’s thrown the thing at my face, which was super gross, by the way, he was covered in sweat, and then he runs off. I get a little confused, because he didn’t exactly have a reason to get half naked, and he was also running towards the rooftop. And I know because I ended up chasing him there. Up every. Single. Staircase. If I’d known that’s where he was going in the first place, I would have taken the damn elevator. Which I didn’t get to do, because five floors from the top he beat me to it and took it instead.”

“I know. Needless to say, I was pretty pissed by the time I got there. Hell, I get a little angry just thinking about it.” Her fingers curl inward, as if strangling an invisible neck. “But it didn’t matter anymore, because he was on the roof, and I was on the roof, and I had him cornered. Again. This time, I learned my lesson and didn’t stand close enough for him to throw his pants at me too.”
You "+({var0}.isBimbo()?"giggle":{var1}.isBro()?"guffaw":"laugh")+" at the image despite yourself. Evening’s face, on the other hand, only portrays pain.
context: <<POS:02034811>> <<VARS:var0=pc,var1=pc>> output("\n\nYou "+(pc.isBimbo()?"giggle":pc.isBro()?"guffaw":"laugh")+" at the image despite yourself. Evening’s face, on the other hand, only portrays pain.")

original: “At this point, I’m still trying to play good cop, because I’d figured he went up there to jump and I would have lost some bonus pay if he died. Did the whole ‘death isn’t worth it, you can still make amends’ spiel. But he doesn’t listen to me. He jumps,” Evening leans in, voice lowered to a conspiratorial whisper. “But he doesn’t fall.”
Your eyes widen. “What do you mean?”
“I ran over to the edge, expecting to see him make a human-sized mess on the concrete, but instead I watched him sprout wings in mid-air. Ended up finding an empty tube of Dovebalm nearby.”
“So that’s why he took off his shirt.”
Your eyes get a little dreamy as you can’t help but wonder what he must have looked like. Maybe he had a lot of juicy muscles...

“I have to admit,” The mercenary says begrudgingly. “Brilliant bastard or just ballsy as fuck, it was a pretty smart move. I think that’s why he led me on a goosechase up those stairs, too, to give himself enough time for the Dovebalm to kick in.”
That still leaves one question unanswered. “So what happened to him?”
“Did he get away?”
Evening pauses, as if having to recollect how the rest of the story went. “Oh, I just shot him,” she says, entirely too nonchalant.
“...What? That’s it?”

“Well, yeah. He gained the ability to fly, not deflect bullets. Or for that matter, turn invisible. It was a smart trick, but he didn’t quite think it through enough to pull off the entire escape. I guess he didn’t count on needing a shield that day. Oops.” The mercenary shrugs. “He broke both his legs in the fall, but I still got my bonus.”
Huh. A somewhat shocking, yet unsurprising ending to that story.
A few drinks later...
“This story takes place many years ago in a galaxy far, far away.” Evening starts, waving her hands towards said unknown galaxy.
You squint at her in suspicion. "+({var0}.isAss()?"“Don’t fuck around.”":"“Somehow I don’t believe you.”")
context: <<POS:02037121>> <<VARS:var0=pc>> output("\n\nYou squint at her in suspicion. "+(pc.isAss()?"“Don’t fuck around.”":"“Somehow I don’t believe you.”"))

original: “Okay, fine, it was six months ago and I was on Uveto.” The mercenary rolls her eyes, dropping the pretense immediately. “My ship needed repairs. That isn’t really new, because it could always use repairs, but I was nearby and figured I could hunt for a bit of treasure and make a quick buck. There are lots of hidden gems if you know where to look, quite literally. I thought it would be a cinch to make a few thousand credits and be on my merry way...” She shivers
, as if feeling the cold Uveto breeze again despite being light years away
. “I was very wrong.”

“Why? Did you forget to buy a heat belt?” You remember what it was like "+({var0}()?"losing your HP every time you wanted to move one goddamn square without one.":"freezing half to death walking on that planet’s surface.")
context: <<POS:02037936>> <<VARS:var0=isSillyModeEnabled>> output("\n\n“Why? Did you forget to buy a heat belt?” You remember what it was like "+(isSillyModeEnabled()?"losing your HP every time you wanted to move one goddamn square without one.":"freezing half to death walking on that planet’s surface."))

original: “Why? Is there something wrong with Uveto?”
“It’s cold as balls is why,” Evening scoffs. “The entire planet is basically covered in one ongoing blizzard. And if you don’t go with the proper supplies on hand, you will freeze to death.”
“So, did you go there prepared?”

“Hey.” Evening’s eyes narrow. “I will have you know I came very prepared. I bought a heat belt. I got climbing gear. I wore pants that covered all of my legs. I made sure to put all the gems I found in a separate bag so the savicite didn’t touch my bare skin and downed a bunch of Chill Pills just in case.” She ticks off every item by the finger, one by one. “I did many things right, but sometimes no matter how much you do right, life can still decide to fuck you over. And in this case, it decided to fuck me over by sending a giant horde of milodan to ambush me.”
“That’s terrible! What happened next?”
“Oof. Bad luck.”
“Wow, sounded like you were fucked.”

“It was not looking good for me, I’ll give you that. There were at least six of them in total. One second I was minding my own business, trekking the Uveto tundra, exploiting it for my own gain, doing absolutely nothing wrong, and the next I see them hulking over the top of a snowbank.” The mercenary mimes the shape of the snowy hill. “It wouldn’t have been a problem if there weren’t so many of them. One or two, I could definitely handle. Hell, maybe even three. But by this point, I was running low on supplies, and even if I’d shot down the first half as quickly as possible, I was pretty sure the latter would have made me into mincemeat. I’m not proud to say this, but I just ran. Unfortunately, they all started running after me.”
You ask her how she got away. In response, Evening bites her lip and looks unexpectedly nervous.

“Technically, I didn’t,” she confesses. “They were all hot on my tail and between the cold and ice, I was one slip away from tripping, eating shit, and then getting caught.” Her lips press together at the prospect. “I needed something that could distract them. You and Evening start off timing each shot with care, only for the game to devolve into the two of you whipping dart after dart at the targets in a frenzy. It’s no longer a game: it is a war. “Damn,” your opponent swears as you whoop in adrenaline-fueled victory. “You gave me a run for my money, Steele!” While she rubs the back of her neck in a posture of defeat, the smile on her face is undeniably genuine. “Shit, a loss is a loss, but I haven’t had that much fun getting my ass beat in ages.” The woman laughs as she wires you your hard-earned spoils. “Don’t spend it all in one place, okay? Evening’s score is just a few points higher than yours. So close!

Your opponent whoops in adrenaline-fueled glee before slapping you on the back of the shoulder. “Shit, that was close!” she says breathlessly. “You gave me a run for my money there! “I think I’ll leave getting my ass whooped again for another day,” you ultimately decide.

A woman with dark hair and what looks to be a very souped-up utility belt saunters over to you. “Looking for an opponent?” She wastes no time in propositioning you, jerking her head towards the dartboard projectors. “Technically, you could play alone, but if you ask me these things are more fun with some friendly competition.” Her grin is a little wolfish in nature, but not predatory. “I’m Evening, by the way.”

You exchange names before the woman runs a hand over the projector’s switch. “Have you used this thing before? It’s got a couple of different settings to it. I can explain them to you if you need it.”

“Ooooh, you found the dartboard!” Evening pops up beside you, having apparently wandered over. “I’ve been itching to play recently!” Noticing your interest, she runs a hand over the projector. “Have you used this thing before? It’s got a couple of different settings to it. I can explain them to you if you need it.”
“Changed your mind about playing?” Evening whispers into your ear, having apparently been summoned forth by some dartboard-related sixth sense. “My offer still stands. I’ll even go easy on you!”
You
pout petulantly
raise a very doubtful eyebrow
narrow your eyes in suspicion
at her.
“...Okay, I won’t. But still, a thousand credits!” she tries to tempt you in a sing-songy voice. “And maybe you’ll get lucky against me. Very, very lucky.”

“Up for a rematch?” Evening whispers into your ear, having apparently been summoned forth by some dartboard-related sixth sense. She sounds slightly more sinister than usual. “Don’t think I haven’t forgotten how you actually beat me last time. I have a score to settle with you, Steele. A thousand credits to win back. A reputation to uphold.”
You scoff lightly. “Your reputation as the person who wins all the dart games?”
“Well, when you put it that way, it sounds silly,” Evening scoffs right back. “But yes, that’s exactly what I’m talking about. So, how about it?”
“Up for a rematch?” Evening whispers into your ear, having apparently been summoned forth by some dartboard-related sixth sense.
“Void,” you swear, veering sideways to reclaim some of the personal space the Terran had so abruptly invaded. “Do you sneak up on people like that every time you want a match?”
“...no, why?” She’s clearly lying. “Sorry, I just get... excited when it comes to throwing sharp things. Don’t judge me!”
“That ship has sailed light years past us.”

Now it’s the mercenary’s turn to become offended, as she puffs and huffs, her cheeks blowing out at the indignity. “Fine, then consider this an official challenge from me. I’m gonna win those credits off you so I can buy back my dignity.”
“That’s not how credits-”
“Just shut up and fight me, Steele.”
You press your lips together, deep in thought as you are. “I dunno. You kicked my ass last time and I don’t exactly like giving my money away. Unless you’re going to go easy on me this time?”
“Ehhhhh- no, probably not,” the Terran confesses with a sheepish shrug. “But you might get lucky! Really, really lucky. Come oooon, it’s too lonely playing all by myself. Do it for little ol’ me?”

You tell her that you haven’t. “No problem; I can run through them real quick,” Evening says genially. She flips through the settings one by one. “First one’s just a Terran dartboard, nothing fancy. You throw, you score, and you try to score higher than your opponent. Second one,” she says as she switches to the setting with the multiple boards, “is a lot trickier. I think the Gryvain made this one up. Your darts bounce off the green boards; they only stick to the blue one. You have to bounce your dart off a certain number of boards each round before it can land.” And then the third one, with the changing shapes. “This one, I think the Zaika thought of. Hit the blue things. Don’t hit the red ones. Beware of moving objects or changing shapes.” She smiles. “Got all that?”
You nod, before your opponent-to-be rubs her hands together conspiratorially.
“I’m good,” you tell her. Evening smiles, rubbing her hands together conspiratorially.
“How about we make this interesting? One thousand credits say I can beat you at any game of your choosing. What’d you say?”
